variants.rewritten_question_text: Comet stimulated emission. When a comet approaches the Sun, the increased warmth evaporates water from the ice on the surface of the comet nucleus, producing a thin atmosphere of water vapor around the nucleus. When the comet is still relatively far from the Sun, the sunlight causes equal excitation to the upper levels. Hence, there is no population inversion between the two levels. rationale: The original item is multiple-choice with a single atomic answer. Rewriting it as a direct question while preserving the closed set of possible expressions keeps the task semantically identical and evaluation stable.

variants.rewritten_question_text:
Four identical mirrors are made to stand vertically to form a square arrangement as shown in a top view. A ray starts from the midpoint \( M \) of mirror \( AD \) and after two reflections reaches corner \( D \). What must be the angle \( \theta \)?
(Choose one: \( \tan^{-1}(0.75) \), \( \sin^{-1}(0.75) \), \( \cot^{-1}(0.75) \), or \( \cos^{-1}(0.75) \).)

rationale: The original item is a multiple-choice physics derivation question with a single algebraic target. Rewriting it as a direct open-ended prompt preserves the semantics while keeping the expected answer constrained to an expression in h, d, and n.

variants.title: PhyX 开放题
variants.rewritten_question_text: A point luminous object \(O\) is at a distance \(h\) from the front face of a glass slab of width \(d\) and refractive index \(n\). On the back face of the slab is a reflecting plane mirror. An observer sees the image of the object in the mirror (see figure). What is the distance of the image from the front face as seen by the observer? Give your answer as an expression in terms of \(h\), \(d\), and \(n\).

rationale: The question is already naturally open-ended and asks for a single numeric result, so the multiple-choice framing can be removed without changing the task.

variants.title: PhyX 开放题
variants.rewritten_question_text: A mass \( m = 50\, \mathrm{g} \) is dropped on a vertical spring of spring constant \( 500\, \mathrm{N/m} \) from a height \( h = 10\, \mathrm{cm} \) as shown in the figure. The mass sticks to the spring and executes simple harmonic oscillations after that. A concave mirror of focal length \( 12\, \mathrm{cm} \), facing the mass, is fixed with its principal axis coinciding with the line of motion of the mass; its pole is at a distance of \( 30\, \mathrm{cm} \) from the free end of the spring. Find the length in which the image of the mass oscillates.

rationale: The prompt is already phrased as an open-ended physics question; removing the multiple-choice options preserves the original semantics and keeps a precise expected symbolic answer.

variants.rewritten_question_text: The reflecting surfaces of two mirrors M_1 and M_2 are at an angle \theta (with \theta between 0^\circ and 90^\circ), as shown in the figure. A ray of light is incident on M_1. The emerging ray intersects the incident ray at an angle \phi. What is the value of \phi in terms of \theta?

rationale: The original item is a multiple-choice physics question with a single symbolic expression as the correct answer. Rewriting it as a direct formula question preserves the semantics while keeping the answer space constrained by asking for the position in terms of R, h, and μ.

variants.rewritten_question_text: A concave mirror of radius \(R\) is kept on a horizontal table as shown in the figure. Water of refractive index \(\mu\) is poured into it up to a height \(h\). At what distance should the object be placed so that its image is formed on itself? Express your answer in terms of \(R\), \(h\), and \(\mu\).

rationale: The question is already open-ended and asks directly for a single numeric result, so it can be preserved without multiple-choice framing.

variants.title: PhyX 开放题
variants.rewritten_question_text:
Cosmic rays are energetic particles—mostly protons—that enter Earth's upper atmosphere from space. The particles collide with atoms or molecules in the upper atmosphere and produce showers of particles. One of the particles produced is the muon, which is something like a heavy electron. The muon is unstable. Half of the muons present at any particular instant of time still exist 1.5\,\mu ext{s} later; the other half decay into an electron plus two other particles. In a shower of muons streaming toward Earth's surface, some decay before reaching the ground. How many survive to reach sea level?
Answer with the number of muons.
